messages.content:
You are a GDPR compliance analyst. When given a document excerpt, evaluate it step by step against each GDPR requirement. For each step, quote the relevant text if found, or state it is missing. End with a structured verdict listing all violations. Always follow this exact format:

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): ...
Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): ...
Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): ...
Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): ...
Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): ...
Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): ...
Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): ...
Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): ...
Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): ...
Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): ...

VERDICT: [Compliant / Non-Compliant / Partially Compliant]
VIOLATIONS: [list each article breached]
RECOMMENDATIONS: [one line per fix needed]

Assess the following healthcare app privacy notice for GDPR compliance:

"HealthTrack collects your name, date of birth, symptoms, diagnoses, and medication history to provide personalised health recommendations. We obtain your explicit consent before collecting any health information. You may withdraw consent at any time by deleting your account. Data is stored on servers in Frankfurt, Germany. We retain your health data for 5 years to enable longitudinal health tracking. You have the right to access, correct, or delete your data by contacting support@healthtrack.com. Our Data Protection Officer can be reached at dpo@healthtrack.com. In the event of a data breach, we will notify the relevant supervisory authority within 72 hours and affected users within 7 days."

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): [PASS] Explicit consent stated. "We obtain your explicit consent before collecting any health information" satisfies Art. 6(1)(a).

Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): [PASS] Health data (symptoms, diagnoses, medication history) is correctly identified as special category data, and explicit consent (Art. 9(2)(a)) is obtained prior to collection.

Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): [PASS] Clearly stated. "Personalised health recommendations" is a specific, explicit purpose. Longitudinal tracking is also disclosed as a secondary purpose with its own justification.

Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): [PASS] Reasonable. Name, DOB, symptoms, diagnoses, and medications are proportionate to the stated purpose.

Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): [PASS] "5 years" is a defined retention period with a stated rationale (longitudinal tracking).

Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): [PARTIAL] Rights to access (Art. 15), rectification (Art. 16), and erasure (Art. 17) are mentioned. Rights to portability (Art. 20), restriction (Art. 18), and objection (Art. 21) are absent. Withdrawal of consent is addressed via account deletion, but this conflates two separate actions.

Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): [FAIL] Not addressed. No mention of whether any processors handle the data.

Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): [PASS] "Servers in Frankfurt, Germany" confirms EEA-based storage. No international transfer concerns.

Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): [PASS] DPO disclosed. Contact provided at dpo@healthtrack.com.

Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): [PASS] 72-hour supervisory authority notification and 7-day user notification both stated.

VERDICT: Partially Compliant
VIOLATIONS: Art. 15-21 (incomplete rights — portability, restriction, objection missing), Art. 28 (no processor disclosure)
RECOMMENDATIONS:
- Add data portability right (Art. 20) with a mechanism to export data in machine-readable format
- Add rights to restriction (Art. 18) and objection (Art. 21) with contact instructions
- Separate consent withdrawal from account deletion
- Disclose any third-party processors and confirm DPAs are in place
